focus: Resolve seam continuity with minimal edits.
next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 3

The shell path bit at Mina's ankles with roots and broken oyster as soon as she stepped off the screened walkway toward the county dock. Behind her the camp had gone from evening noise to the thinner sounds people made when they were pretending to sleep in heat. A box fan knocked somewhere in the guest rooms, once per rotation, the casing loose enough to complain but not loose enough to stop. The generator kept its low metal headache behind the shed. Beyond the pilings, the dark had weight. It held the channels flat and hidden, and it held the rookery too, spoonbills muttering in the mangroves with the ugly intimate sound of birds settling on eggs and each other.

She had changed into the least reflective clothes she owned, black shorts, dark long-sleeved shirt despite the heat, old deck shoes she did not mind ruining. Bug dope slicked the backs of her knees. She had a red-filter flashlight in one pocket, a small notebook and golf pencil in a zip bag in the other, and no camera worth the shame of carrying. If she came back with nothing, a camera would only turn the whole night into a better joke. If she came back with something, a camera phone clip through mangrove dark would not save her name.

Mosquitoes found the strip of skin above her collar and needled there until she slapped one and felt her own blood wet on her fingertip. She kept walking.

Tomas was at the fuel cage under the dock light, half in yellow glare and half out of it. He had a penlight clenched in his teeth and a pair of cheap reading glasses low on his nose while he copied a number from the pump into the district ledger line he had left blank the night before. The county shirt hung open over a sweat-dark tank. His forearms were already striped with bites, and every time a mosquito landed...
prev_tail_excerpt:
...el could save anything. She shut her eyes until the memory thinned. What had ruined her once did not stop wanting her. That was the ugliest part. The penalty and the gossip hurt, but what shamed her more was wanting it still.

Out in the dark, the airboat moved off. She could hear it by its changing pressure on the night, the engine swallowed and returned by bends in the creek.

Mina opened her eyes.

Imogen could have the daylight version. She could have the clean shirts, the donor voice, and the woman on the platform who knew where to point binoculars and how to make retirees feel chosen by a bird. Mina would carry bait, fold linens, smile through the heat, and make herself worth the bunk.

She was staying if the rumor carried even half the weight it had taken on in her body. She would learn the camp's rhythms, the boardwalk to the county dock, the timing of tides and guest dinners, and who locked what where. She would find out when Tomas filed invoices, when the county boat came back light, whether the fuel-cage key ever left his belt, whether Imogen trusted her enough to send her with paperwork or ice. The line of his mouth kept surfacing in the same thought as those questions, and that made the planning uglier than she wanted to admit.

The fan rattled once in the window and nearly stopped. Mina kicked the wall near the floor. It caught again and lurched back into motion.

When the airboat noise had thinned to almost nothing, she was still standing there, listening like she could hear blue through wood and water both. By the time she lay down, the decision had settled in her with the hot, mean steadiness of a hook set clean. Morning could have the office version of this: forms, permits, whatever paper might get her near the west channels without setting off alarms.
